1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	\label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).
78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open, lem:rigidity_core, lem:rigidity_snd_lift}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}

134	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,

138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}

216	\begin{lemma}[Cartesian-monoidal collapse identity]
217	  \label{lem:rigidity_snd_lift}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_snd_lift}
219	  Let \(X\), \(Y\) be objects over \(\bar k\) and fix a \(\bar k\)-point \(x_0\) of \(X\).
220	  Post-composing the second projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) with the slice section
221	  \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) equals the ``collapse the \(X\)-coordinate onto \(x_0\)'' endomorphism
222	  \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) of \(X \times Y\). This is pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no
223	  geometry): the product universal property distributes \(p_2\), the \(Y\)-component simplifies by
224	  the identity law, and the \(X\)-component collapses by uniqueness of maps to the terminal object.
225	\end{lemma}
226	\begin{lemma}[Bridge~1: completeness makes the projection a closed map]
227	  \label{lem:snd_left_isClosedMap}
228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.snd_left_isClosedMap}
229	  \uses{lem:projectiveLineBar_isProper}
230	  Let \(X\) be complete (proper) over \(\bar k\) and \(Y\) any object over \(\bar k\). Then the
231	  underlying topological map of the second projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
232	  \emph{closed} map. This is Mumford's ``completeness of \(X\) makes \(p_2\) a closed map'': the
233	  projection is the base change of \(X \to \Spec \bar k\) along \(Y \to \Spec \bar k\), properness
234	  of \(X\) gives universal closedness, and universal closedness is stable under base change, so the
235	  base-changed projection is universally closed and hence a closed map. Characteristic-free; no
236	  theorem of the cube, no cohomology.
237	\end{lemma}
238	\begin{lemma}[Scheme-level gluing core of the Rigidity Lemma]
239	  \label{lem:rigidity_core}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_core}
241	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
242	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed, \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible,
243	  reduced and locally of finite type over \(\bar k\), and \(Z\) separated. Let
244	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) collapse the slice \(X \times \{y_0\}\) to a single point \(z_0\).
245	  Then \(f\) equals its composite with the collapse endomorphism \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\), i.e.\
246	  \(f = \mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\). The proof takes the non-empty open \(U\) on which \(f\) and
247	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}); since \(X \times Y\)
248	  is geometrically irreducible over the one-point base \(\Spec \bar k\), it is irreducible, so the
249	  non-empty open \(U\) is dense and its inclusion is dominant; the dominant-source/separated-target
250	  rigidity handle then promotes agreement on \(U\) to equality of the two morphisms everywhere.
251	\end{lemma}
252	\begin{lemma}
253	\leanok
254	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
255	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
257	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, lem:snd_left_isClosedMap}
258	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
260	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
261	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
262	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
263	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
264	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
265	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
266	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
267	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
268	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
269	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
270	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
271	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
272	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
273	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
274	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
275	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
276	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
277	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
278	  \medskip
279	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
280	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
281	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
282	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
283	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
284	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
285	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
286	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
287	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
288	  \medskip
289	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
290	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
291	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
292	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
293	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
294	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
295	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
296	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
297	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
298	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
299	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
300	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
301	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
302	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
303	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
304	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
305	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
306	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
307	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
308	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
309	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
310	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
311	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
312	\end{lemma}

324	  \leanok
325	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
326	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
327	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
328	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
329	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
330	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
331	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
332	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
333	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
334	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
335	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
336	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
337	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
338	  \medskip
339	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
340	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
341	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
342	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
343	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
344	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
345	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
346	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
347	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
348	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
349	  proven.
350	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
351	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
352	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
353	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
354	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
355	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
356	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
357	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
358	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
359	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely

399	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
400	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite

409	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
410	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
411	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
412	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
413	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
414	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
415	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
416	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
417	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
418	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
419	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
420	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
421	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
422	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
423	  endomorphism. Suppose:
424	  \begin{itemize}
425	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
426	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
427	      its points);
428	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
429	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
430	  \end{itemize}
431	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
432	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
433	\end{lemma}
434	\begin{proof}
435	  \leanok
436	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
437	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
438	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
439	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
440	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
441	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
442	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.

444	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
445	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
446	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
447	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
448	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
449	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
450	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
451	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
452	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
453	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
454	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
455	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
456	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
457	  \medskip
458	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
459	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
460	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
461	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
462	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
463	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
464	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
465	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
466	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
467	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
468	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
469	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
470	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
471	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
472	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
473	\end{proof}

476	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (\cref{def:depth} and \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (\cref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (\cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (\cref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65

67	  [Depth of a module]

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:

41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/DualInverse.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/PresheafInternalHom.lean
8	
9	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
10	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
11	
12	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
13	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
14	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
15	\[
16	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
17	  \qquad
18	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
19	\]
20	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
21	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
22	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
23	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
24	
25	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
26	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
27	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
28	% groups)
29	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
30	% SOURCE QUOTE:
31	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
32	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
33	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
34	% abelian groups defined by the formula
35	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
36	% \end{dfn}
37	%  [\dots]
38	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
39	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
41	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
42	% topologies  by
43	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
44	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
45	%  \end{dfn}"
46	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
47	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
48	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
49	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
50	homomorphisms.}
51	
52	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
53	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
54	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
55	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
56	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
57	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
58	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
59	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
60	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
61	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
62	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
63	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
64	structure. This is the content of
65	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
66	
67	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
68	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
69	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
70	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
71	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
72	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
73	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
74	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
75	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
76	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
77	\begin{enumerate}
78	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
79	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
80	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on

1	\chapter{Rigidity over a base field \(k\): morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve to a group scheme are constant}
2	\label{chap:RigidityKbar}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityKbar.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157): The project's COMMITTED genus-0 rigidity route is now route
6	% (c) --- the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity-lemma stack developed in its own
7	% chapter \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (Lean file
8	% AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, upstream of Jacobian.lean). The headline
9	% there, thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV (char-free, [IsAlgClosed k-bar] only), is what
10	% genusZeroWitness consumes. THIS chapter now documents the over-k-bar declaration
11	% rigidity_over_kbar (which carries [CharZero k-bar]) together with the FALLBACK route (a):
12	% the df=0 / cotangent-triviality + Serre-duality decomposition and the closed, axiom-clean
13	% chart-algebra envelope (which supplies only the converse "df=0 implies constant"). Route
14	% (a) is retained in tree, off the critical path; the df=0 framing below is the fallback,
15	% NOT the committed route.
16	
17	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
18	
19	\begin{quote}
20	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
21	\end{quote}
22	
23	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
24	
25	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
26	
27	\begin{itemize}
28	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
29	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
30	\end{itemize}
31	
32	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
33	
34	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
35	\begin{itemize}
36	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
37	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
38	\end{itemize}
39	
40	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.
41	
42	\textbf{The Lean declaration's signature now carries \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}} (matching the theorem's existing docstring, which already described \(\bar k\) as algebraically closed --- the hypothesis was simply absent from the formal statement). Prior strategy iterations had instead routed through base-change to \(\bar k\) followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c, \(\sim 4\)--\(8\) iter / \(300\)--\(500\) LOC); the iter-127 framing then claimed all three pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build over an \emph{arbitrary} base field, dropping descent entirely. The alg-closed pivot supersedes that framing for the rigidity theorem itself: rigidity is established over \(\bar k\), and the descent of the constancy conclusion to a general \(k\) is a short downstream consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (faithfully-flat covers are epimorphisms of schemes), with the detailed descent living in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter. The group-scheme cotangent-triviality pieces (i)/(iii) below remain genuinely intrinsic to the group scheme and are stated over a general base where that is sound; only the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (the KDM/constants core) requires the alg-closed base.
43	
44	\section{Statement}
45	
46	% NOTE: classical rigidity for morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1\) (or genus-0
47	% pointed proper curves) to abelian / group varieties; standard
48	% references are Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch.~II \S4 (rational
49	% curves on abelian varieties; paywalled, not bundled) and Hartshorne,
50	% *Algebraic Geometry*, Ex.~IV.4 (not bundled in references/). The
51	% cotangent-route closure used here ties into Stacks Tag 04QM (smooth
52	% over fields ⇒ geometrically reduced; references/stacks-varieties.tex,
53	% lemma-smooth-geometrically-normal) for the chart-level differential
54	% machinery.
55	\begin{theorem}
56	  \label{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar}
58	  \leanok
59	  \uses{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy,
60	    lem:GrpObj_omega_free, lem:GrpObj_omega_rank_eq_dim,
61	    lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace, lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank,
62	    lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero,
63	    lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart,
64	    lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero,
65	    lem:constants_integral_over_base_field,
66	    thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact}
67	  % SOURCE: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4 (verbatim text not yet
68	  % retrieved — paywalled, no open copy in references/). No SOURCE QUOTE
69	  % is supplied: the source is not bundled and recalled text must not be
70	  % substituted (citation discipline).
71	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, II §4.}
72	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (Lean: \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}; in the use case, the algebraic closure of a base field \(k\), with descent of the conclusion to \(k\) handled downstream in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). These two hypotheses match the theorem's existing docstring and are required by the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}, \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}); see \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot". Let \(C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\) (i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\)). Let \(A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta_A \in A(\bar k)\). Let
73	  \[
74	    f \colon C \longrightarrow A
75	  \]
76	  be a morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes over \(\Spec \bar k\), and let \(p \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point such that \(f(p) = \eta_A\). Then \(f\) equals the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\):
77	  \[
78	    f \;=\; \bigl(C \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\bigr).
79	  \]
80

253:  \item[(iv)] \emph{Serre duality on a smooth proper curve --- \textbf{DEFERRED named gap, ON the C.2.d critical path (iter-155 correction)}.} The honest-closure verdict (\texttt{analogies/serre-duality.md}, iter-110) is \textbf{3000--8000 LOC}. \textbf{Correction (iter-155).} Earlier text here claimed piece (iv) was used ``by the M2.d-alt genus-\(0\) identification alternative, NOT by C.2.d itself'' and that ``C.2.d / M2.a body closure does NOT depend on piece (iv)''. \emph{That is false.} The iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that the C.2.d keystone reduces to producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), which is a global-sections fact: with the gap-(i) trivialisation \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}\), one has \(\mathrm df \in \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) --- which is exactly the genus-\(0\) instance of this Serre-duality piece (iv). Hence piece (iv) (or an equivalent that yields \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)) is \textbf{on} the C.2.d critical path under the cotangent-bundle closure route (a); only the dual-AV route (b) can in principle bypass it, by amortising the \(\Pic^0\) engine. The chart-by-chart KDM stack does \emph{not} avoid this input (see the correction in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}). Piece (iv) remains a deferred named gap; it is not opened on a prover lane until a closure route is committed.
266:  \item Piece (i.b) Step 2 d\_app sub-sorry inside \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj}'s proof, the IsIso theorem \cref{lem:GrpObj_basechange_along_proj_two_inv_app_isIso} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_app\_isIso}), and piece (i.b) Main \cref{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) are \textbf{DESCOPED from critical-path}. \emph{Status correction (iter-155):} \texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent} was subsequently \textbf{EXCISED from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}, \textbf{not} a present declaration. This excised gap-(i) globalisation (\(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC) is exactly the gap (i) that the keystone \(\mathrm df = 0\) (chapter introduction) is gated on; the blueprint blocks below for these declarations are therefore an off-path record of the bundled-route design, not a live in-tree obligation.
272:Piece (i.a) --- \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}) and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}), closed iter-128 \(\to\) iter-132 --- remains in-tree as standalone infrastructure. Chart-algebra does \emph{not} consume it on the rigidity critical path, but the cotangent-space-at-the-identity definition and its rank are independently useful downstream (Lie-algebra dual \(\mathfrak g\) on the tangent side, etc.).
282:      \item \textbf{Constancy-from-\(\mathrm df = 0\) layer (the converse only).} \emph{Iter-155 correction.} This layer was previously titled the ``No-Serre-duality layer'' and claimed the chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) chain ``replaces'' the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) route, ``eliminating piece (iv) as a critical-path dependency''. \textbf{That claim is false} (per \texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) and is corrected here. What the chain actually delivers is the \emph{converse} implication: \textbf{given} the chart-uniform input \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) (which is the chart-level shadow of the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\)), the conclusion ``\(f\) factors through \(\Spec k\)'' follows from the integrally-closed-constants helper (next item) and the scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} (final item). It does \emph{not} produce the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\), and it does not avoid the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) input: that input is exactly what is needed to \emph{establish} \(\mathrm df = 0\) in the first place. Concretely, \(\mathrm df = 0\) is a global-sections fact --- with the gap-(i) trivialisation \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/k} \cong \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}\), \(\mathrm df \in \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) --- and the chart-by-chart machinery cannot detect it because \(\Omega_{C/k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero) on every affine chart \(V\). Hence piece (iv) (or an equivalent yielding \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\)) is \textbf{on} the critical path for the keystone, not eliminated by this chain.
291:\paragraph{Iter-128 / iter-129 prover lane re-scoping (per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} + iter-129 rename).} Sub-step (i.a) was originally a 2-target pair and is now (post-iter-129) a 3-target trio: \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (the \emph{definition} of \(\mathfrak g^\vee\), plus the finitely-generated-free claim — iter-128 lane, body landed), \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} (the \emph{bridge} to the local-ring cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}\) — iter-130+ build target), and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (the \emph{rank-equals-relative-dimension} claim — iter-130+ build target). The iter-128 prover lane attacks ONLY the first: it scaffolds the Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} (renamed iter-129 from the placeholder \texttt{lieAlgebra}; signature relaxed to a free \texttt{\{n : \mathbb N\} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} binder so downstream consumers of arbitrary relative dimension can instantiate it) and closes its body. The bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} is vestigial on the live path under Replacement (B) (see \S Q4 collapse footer at the rank-lemma proof's end) and remains deferred indefinitely; the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} is the iter-132 prover-lane target (closure path: Step 1 chart-side Kähler rank from \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}'s existential + Step 2 base-change to \(k\) via \texttt{Module.finrank\_baseChange}, both detailed in that lemma's proof body). Rationale: (i) bundling definition + bridge + rank into one prover-iter task is borderline-too-ambitious per \texttt{progress-critic-iter128}; (ii) the rank-lemma RHS Lean encoding needs blueprint pinning iter-129 before being prover-ready (it must read \texttt{Module.finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n} parameterised by an explicit \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} instance; the bare \(\dim G\) in the prose does not type-check directly because Mathlib has no \(\dim G\) function on \texttt{Over (Spec k)}, per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} CHALLENGE on (i.a) signature ambiguity).

This is informational here; the rank lemma above closes without invoking it.
482:  \emph{Step 3 (alternative canonical route, currently deferred).} An alternative closure via the stalk-side cotangent space \(\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}\) is available in principle and was the iter-128-era plan. The route is: (i) bridge from the chart-base-changed body to the local-stalk Kähler module via \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} (currently \texttt{\textbackslash notready}; identifies \(k \otimes_{\Gamma(G, V)} \Omega_{\Gamma(G, V)/\Gamma(\Spec k, U)} \cong \mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}\), via a localisation-at-the-identity-stalk step composed with the standard \(k \otimes_{R} \Omega_{R/k} \cong \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}\) identification — Stacks Tag~02G1 / Hartshorne~II.8.7); (ii) invoke regularity of \(\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}\) (\texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}; from smoothness of \(G \to \Spec k\) at the closed point \(\eta_G\), Stacks Tag~00TR / Hartshorne~II.10.3.B), with Krull dimension of \(\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}\) equal to the relative dimension \(n\) (Stacks Tag~02G1); (iii) read off the rank from the defining property of regular local rings, \(\finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2} = \dim \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G} = n\), packaged into \texttt{Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Cotangent}) and \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.RegularLocalRing.Defs}). 1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
19	  
20	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
21	  \[
22	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
23	  \]
24	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
25	\end{definition}
26	
27	\begin{proof}
28	  
29	  \leanok
30	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
31	
32	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
33	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
34	    \[
35	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
36	    \]
37	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
38	  \end{remark}
39	
40	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
41	\end{proof}
42	
43	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
44	\begin{itemize}
45	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
46	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
47	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
48	\end{itemize}
49	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
50	
51	\section{Mathlib gap}
52	\label{sec:Genus_status}
53	
54	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
58	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
59	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
60	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.

2250	\begin{lemma}
2251	[(S3.sep.2) Geometrically reduced finite field extension is separable]
2252	  \label{lem:S3_sep_2_geom_reduced_finite_field_ext_is_separable}
2253	  \lean{Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite}
2254	  
2255	  % NOTE (iter-152, alg-closed pivot): DESCOPED under the alg-closed
2256	  % pivot — general-over-k Mathlib-PR fodder, NOT on the M2.a critical
2257	  % path (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} no longer uses
2258	  % it). Label + statement retained for the general-over-k route.
2259	  Let \(F / k\) be a finite field extension of fields. If \(F\) is geometrically reduced as a \(k\)-algebra (\texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced}\,\(k\)\,\(F\), equivalently \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is reduced), then \(F / k\) is separable (\texttt{Algebra.IsSeparable}\,\(k\)\,\(F\)).
2260	
2261	  This sub-claim is the (S3.sep.2) closer of path (b)'s separability strand: the (S3.sep.1) gateway delivers geometric reducedness of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) over \(k\); combined with finite-field-extension structure (from steps (a)--(d) of path (a) applied at the geometric level), this lemma upgrades geometric reducedness to separability of the finite field extension \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) / k\).
2262	\end{lemma}
2263	
2264	\begin{proof}
2265	  
2266	  By definition \texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced}\,\(k\)\,\(F\) says \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is reduced. Since \(F / k\) is finite, \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra, hence Artinian, and decomposes uniquely as a finite product \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \prod_i F_i\) of Artinian local \(\bar k\)-algebras at its (finitely many) minimal primes. Reducedness of the product forces each Artinian local factor \(F_i\) to be a field, finite over \(\bar k\); algebraic closedness of \(\bar k\) then forces \(F_i = \bar k\).
2267	
2268	  Therefore \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \bar k^{r}\) for \(r = \#\{\text{minimal primes of } F \otimes_k \bar k\}\). The \(\bar k\)-dimension on both sides gives \([F : k] = \dim_{\bar k}(F \otimes_k \bar k) = r\). On the other hand, \(r\) equals the number of distinct \(k\)-algebra embeddings \(F \hookrightarrow \bar k\) (the closed points of \(\Spec(F \otimes_k \bar k)\)). The equality \([F : k] = \#\{\text{embeddings } F \hookrightarrow \bar k\}\) is precisely the criterion for separability of a finite field extension. Estimated build: \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC.

2331	  Cover \(X\) by finitely many affine opens \(U_i = \Spec A_i\) (quasi-compactness from properness). The \v Cech complex on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) computes \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) as the equalizer

2334	  \]
2335	  Tensoring with \(K\) over \(k\) preserves equalizers (flatness of \(K\) as a \(k\)-module). Chart-by-chart, the affine-side flat-base-change isomorphism \(A_i \otimes_k K \cong \Gamma((U_i)_K, \mathcal O_{(U_i)_K})\) holds by Stacks Tag~\textbf{00DS} (flat base change for affines), and similarly for the intersections \(A_{ij} \otimes_k K \cong \Gamma((U_i \cap U_j)_K, \mathcal O)\) (each \(U_i \cap U_j\) is itself quasi-affine and admits a finite affine cover whose flat-base-change identification reassembles to the intersection's flat-base-change isomorphism). Reassembling chart-by-chart with the same \v Cech equalizer description on the base-changed cover \(\{(U_i)_K\}\) identifies \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k K\) with \(\Gamma(X_K, \mathcal O_{X_K})\). Estimated build: \(\sim 150\)--\(250\) LOC (the dominant cost is the \v Cech equaliser + flat-tensor exchange chase, plus the verification that the affine intersection flat-base-change isomorphism reassembles to the global comparison).

Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed, that field equals \(\bar k\) itself. Therefore \((F \otimes_k \bar k) / \mathrm{Nil} \cong \bar k\), so \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a \emph{local} Artinian \(\bar k\)-algebra with residue field \(\bar k\).

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
80

\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
% \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
% references/stacks-coherent.tex).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for

\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
% references/stacks-coherent.md).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
out of scope here.

Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
\[
  \begin{array}{ccc}
    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
  \end{array}
\]
Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback

=== blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex ===

This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of

Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the

properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.

content: 68:  \emph{Classical description.} The classical proof of the same statement proceeds in two parts. In positive genus (\(g(C) > 0\)), the existence and uniqueness of the factorisation come from the Pic-scheme representability theorem together with the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, Route~A (sub-steps A.1--A.4) for the line-bundle / universal-property route via \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), and Route~B (sub-steps B.1--B.3) for the symmetric-power / Stein-factorisation route. In genus \(0\), the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for \(C \to A\) --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}. This rigidity result is established \emph{over the algebraic closure \(\bar k\)}: its signature carries an \(\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k\) hypothesis, and it is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over the algebraically closed base. The resulting equality of morphisms is then descended back to the original base field \(k\) by faithfully-flat descent: because \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) is faithfully flat, two \(k\)-morphisms that agree after base change to \(\bar k\) already agree over \(k\) (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (faithfully-flat descent of morphism equality from \(\bar k\) back to \(k\)) is the descent step carrying the algebraically-closed-base rigidity conclusion down to the original base field; a base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step (establishing rigidity) followed by this descent step is on the M2 critical path. The protected signature of Theorem~REF does \emph{not} assume \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\), so the genus-\(0\) argument branches on whether a \(k\)-rational point exists. When \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\), the supplied \(P \in C(k)\) feeds the pointing hypothesis of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly --- no \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_k\) identification is required, sidestepping the Brauer--Severi obstruction (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexample to an unconditional \(k\)-side identification). When \(C(k) = \emptyset\), the \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\) field of the genus-\(0\) witness (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) is vacuously true at the Lean type level: the universal quantifier \(\forall\, (P \colon \mathbf{1} \to C)\) ranges over the empty type. None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~REF, whose proof is the project's single explicit foundational gap.

For the math delimiter category, there's a consistent pattern of 8 instances appearing across multiple files like AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex, Albanese files, and Picard-related modules, with a few files showing just 2 instances. There's this lemma about separability over finite field extensions that's marked as descoped—it was only potentially connected to a path that got dropped during the algebraic closure pivot, and it's explicitly noted as general-over-k material that's not on the critical path, so it's safe to leave out.

The other one is a purely inseparable lemma with the same situation—no outgoing uses, descoped under the same pivot, and explicitly flagged as Mathlib-PR fodder outside the main critical path.

For both, the real tension is between removal and retention. Wiring them up isn't an option since their only consumer was the dropped assembler. Keeping them would mean accepting isolated blueprints as a valid terminal state, but the strategy doesn't actually track a general-over-k route.
